..summary: Identical inline comment duplicated verbatim in RedisAuthCodeStore

evidence: Lines 41 and 75 both contain the identical comment '// Write prefix depends on JWT mint mode: V2 mints use v2 key prefix, legacy uses legacy prefix'.
The comment restates what the code below it clearly shows — a ternary choosing between V2_KEY_PREFIX and LEGACY_KEY_PREFIX based on JwtMintMode.
detail.suggestion: Remove both inline comments. The method name currentWritePrefix() and the ternary expression are self-documenting.

..summary: Interface Javadoc restates method signatures without adding insight

evidence: AuthCodeStore.storeJwt @return says 'a single-use code that can be exchanged via exchangeCode' — the method name and interface contract already communicate this.
MicrosoftChallengeStore.issueChallenge @return says 'a new MicrosoftChallenge containing the challenge ID and nonce' — this restates the return type.
MicrosoftChallengeStore.consumeNonce has a 7-line Javadoc block that restates the Optional return semantics that the type signature already conveys.
detail.suggestion: Trim interface Javadoc to WHY commentary only. Remove @return tags that restate the return type. Keep @throws tags where the exception contract is non-obvious.

..summary: Warn-level logs on normal disabled-feature-flag configuration states

evidence: Lines 73, 76, 79, 92, 95: five logger.warn() calls for routine states like 'Google client-side login suppressed by rollout flag' and 'Google provider not configured: client ID is missing'.
These fire on every GET /auth/providers call when a provider is intentionally disabled, creating log noise in normal operation.
detail.suggestion: Downgrade to logger.debug() for expected disabled states. Reserve logger.warn() for conditions like 'rollout flag enabled but config missing' which already exists and is appropriate.

..summary: normalizeGoogleClaims takes 5 individual params while normalizeMicrosoftClaims takes a typed record

evidence: normalizeGoogleClaims(String googleId, String email, String name, String pictureUrl, AuthFlow loginFlow) takes 5 individual parameters, while normalizeMicrosoftClaims(MicrosoftIdTokenClaims claims, AuthFlow loginFlow) takes a typed record. Both serve the same role in the normalizer's public API — transforming provider claims to NormalizedIdentity — but use asymmetric parameter styles.
detail.suggestion: Create a VerifiedGoogleIdentity-style typed record for the Google claims (note: GoogleTokenVerifier.VerifiedGoogleIdentity already exists) and change normalizeGoogleClaims to accept (VerifiedGoogleIdentity, AuthFlow), matching the Microsoft overload's shape. The caller in AuthCompletionService.completeGoogleAuthentication already has a VerifiedGoogleIdentity — it just destructures it unnecessarily.

..summary: SecurityConfig authentication entry point writes raw JSON instead of using ErrorResponse DTO

evidence: In SecurityConfig.filterChain(), the authenticationEntryPoint lambda writes '{"error":"Unauthorized"}' as a raw string via response.getWriter().write(), bypassing the ErrorResponse record and Jackson serialization used by all other endpoints
detail.suggestion: Use an ObjectMapper to serialize an ErrorResponse instance, or delegate to a shared method/bean that produces consistent JSON output matching the DTO hierarchy. This keeps all API responses going through the same serialization path.

..summary: LogoutResponse uses 'message' top-level key while ErrorResponse uses 'error' — no discriminator field across sealed AuthApiResponse variants

evidence: ErrorResponse serializes to {"error": "..."} via @JsonProperty("error")
LogoutResponse serializes to {"message": "..."} — plain record field, no annotation
TokenResponse serializes to {"token": "..."}
MicrosoftChallengeResponse serializes to {"challengeId": "...", "nonce": "..."}
Frontend client.ts line 78-80 checks body.error specifically — if LogoutResponse were returned in an error-like context, the frontend would not extract the message
No @JsonTypeInfo or discriminator field exists on the sealed interface to help clients dispatch
detail.suggestion: Either add a shared 'type' discriminator field via @JsonTypeInfo on AuthApiResponse, or standardize LogoutResponse to also use 'message' key on ErrorResponse (renaming @JsonProperty) so success/error responses share a common message key pattern

..summary: POST /auth/logout returns 'Logged out successfully' but performs no server-side JWT invalidation or token blocklisting

evidence: AuthController.java:175-178 — logout() returns ResponseEntity.ok(new LogoutResponse('Logged out successfully')) with no SecurityContext clearing, no token blocklist insertion, no Redis revocation
useAuth.ts:31-34 — frontend logout() only removes JWT from localStorage, meaning the token remains valid until natural expiration
JwtTokenService has no revocation or blocklist mechanism — validateAndExtract only checks signature, issuer, audience, and expiration
detail.suggestion: Either implement a Redis-backed token blocklist (add JTI to a Redis set on logout, check in JwtAuthenticationFilter) or document the endpoint as client-side-only and rename/remove the server-side route to avoid implying server-side invalidation

..summary: apiFetch<T> returns 'undefined as T' for 204 responses, violating its Promise<T> return type

evidence: Line 56: function signature is 'async function apiFetch<T>(path: string, options: RequestInit = {}): Promise<T>'
Line 83-84: 'if (response.status === 204) { return undefined as T; }' — the unsafe cast silently lies about the return type
Comment on line 53-55 acknowledges the issue: 'Note: Returns undefined cast to T for 204 (No Content) responses. Callers expecting 204 should check for undefined explicitly.'
detail.suggestion: Change return type to Promise<T | undefined> or create a separate function for endpoints that may return 204. The current approach pushes type-safety responsibility onto callers via comments rather than the type system.

..summary: UserService.findOrCreateUser silently mutates existing user records despite 'findOrCreate' naming

evidence: Method name is 'findOrCreateUser' suggesting two behaviors: find or create
Lines 36-38: when user is found, delegates to 'updateExistingUser' which overwrites email, name, pictureUrl, provider identity fields, and lastLoginAt
The update-on-find behavior is a hidden side effect — callers (AuthCompletionService line 40) expect find-or-create semantics, not find-update-or-create
detail.suggestion: Rename to 'findOrCreateAndUpdate' or 'syncUser' to make the mutation behavior explicit. Alternatively, add Javadoc documenting that existing users are always updated with latest identity claims on login.

..summary: JwtTokenService.generateToken can throw undocumented IllegalArgumentException in V2 mode

evidence: Line 84: public method 'generateToken(User user)' has no Javadoc or @throws annotation

Line 85-88: delegates to generateV2Token when mint mode is V2, which throws IllegalArgumentException on lines 92-93 if 'user.getId() == null || user.getEmail() == null || user.getProvider() == null || user.getProviderUserId() == null'
In LEGACY mode the method delegates to generateLegacyToken which does not validate inputs, creating mode-dependent preconditions that are invisible to callers
detail.suggestion: Add Javadoc to generateToken documenting that it may throw IllegalArgumentException when JWT mint mode is V2 and user entity is missing required fields (id, email, provider, providerUserId).
